turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito has the opinion of the Court today in Case 15-628 Salman versus United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: This case involves the rules governing liability under Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act and SEC Rule, 10b-5 for those who receive and trade on inside corporate information so called tippees. In Dirks versus SEC decided in 1983, the Court explained that tippee liability depends on whether the tipper who shares inside corporate information with the tippee breached a fiduciary duty by doing so and this in turn depends on whether the tipper disclosed the information for a personal benefit. We further held in Dirks that a personal benefit may be inferred where the tipper “makes a gift” of confidential information to a trading relative or friend. The petitioner in this case, Salman, was convicted for federal securities, fraud crimes for trading on inside information that he received from a friend and relative by marriage, who in turn had received the information from Salman's brother-in-law. Relying on the Second Circuit's opinion in the United States versus Newman, Salman argued to the Ninth Circuit that his conviction could not stand. Although, the original tipper disclosed the information as a gift to his brother, the tipper did not receive anything of monetary or similar value in exchange. The Ninth Circuit disagreed and affirmed the conviction reasoning that under Dirks, the jury could infer a personal benefit because the tipper made “a gift” of confidential information to a trading relative. This case is squarely governed by Dirks. As we explained there, “When an insider makes a gift of confidential information to a trading relative, the tip and trade resemble trading by the insider himself followed by a gift of the profits to the recipient. When a tipper makes a gift of trading information to a relative, the gift is the same thing as trading by the tipper followed by a gift of the proceeds to the family member.” We reason that the tipper personally benefits in both situations. In Dirks we observed that the personal benefit injury will not always be easy for the courts and that certainly remains true, but this case falls in the heartland of Dirks' gift giving rule. For these and other reasons described in our opinion, we affirm the Ninth Circuit's judgment and our decision is unanimous.
